channel.title: Medyan Statistics Blog - R
channel.description: R tutorials, statistical analysis guides, and data visualization tips for academic research

item.title: Kaplan-Meier Survival Analysis in R: Step-by-Step Guide
item.dc:creator: Medyan Statistics
item.description:
Introduction

Survival analysis is one of the essential statistical methods in medical research. It is used to analyze the time until an event occurs (death, recurrence, recovery, etc.).

In this guide, you will learn how to create Kaplan-Meier curves, compare groups with the log-rank test, and produce publication-quality graphics.

Required Packages

Kodu Göster 
library(survival)
library(survminer)

Data Preparation

Let’s create a simulated clinical trial dataset: 200 patients in Drug and Placebo groups.

Kodu Göster 
set.seed(42)
n <- 200

df <- data.frame(
  time = c(rweibull(n/2, shape = 1.2, scale = 24),
           rweibull(n/2, shape = 1.0, scale = 16)),
  status = sample(0:1, n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.25, 0.75)),
  group = rep(c("Drug", "Placebo"), each = n/2)
)

head(df)
       time status group
1  3.198001      0  Drug
2  2.459944      1  Drug
3 28.929355      0  Drug
4  5.902881      1  Drug
5 12.190112      1  Drug
6 16.882902      1  Drug

Kaplan-Meier Curves

Kodu Göster 
# Create survival object
surv_obj <- Surv(time = df$time, event = df$status)

# KM fit
km_fit <- survfit(surv_obj ~ group, data = df)

# Log-rank test
lr_test <- survdiff(surv_obj ~ group, data = df)
p_val <- 1 - pchisq(lr_test$chisq, df = 1)

# Plot
ggsurvplot(
  km_fit,
  data = df,
  pval = TRUE,
  conf.int = TRUE,
  risk.table = TRUE,
  risk.table.col = "strata",
  palette = c("#2E86AB", "#C73E1D"),
  xlab = "Time (months)",
  ylab = "Survival Probability",
  title = "Kaplan-Meier Survival Curves",
  legend.title = "Group",
  legend.labs = c("Drug", "Placebo"),
  ggtheme = theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
)
Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.
ℹ The deprecated feature was likely used in the ggpubr package.
  Please report the issue at <https://github.com/kassambara/ggpubr/issues>.

Interpreting the Results

  • Median survival time: Obtained via summary(km_fit)
  • Log-rank p-value: Is the difference between groups statistically significant?
  • Confidence intervals: The shaded area around the curve represents the 95% CI
Kodu Göster 
# Median survival times
print(km_fit)
Call: survfit(formula = surv_obj ~ group, data = df)

                n events median 0.95LCL 0.95UCL
group=Drug    100     79   20.0   16.88    25.2
group=Placebo 100     74   12.7    8.72    18.4

Tips for Academic Use

  1. Resolution: Save with at least 300 DPI using ggsave()
  2. Risk table: Always include in journal submissions
  3. Censoring: Censored observations are shown with “+” marks
  4. Reporting: Write as “Median survival was X months (95% CI: Y-Z)”

Conclusion

The survival and survminer packages in R make it straightforward to produce publication-quality Kaplan-Meier analyses. Prepare your data, write a few lines of code, and get graphics ready for your manuscript.

For survival analysis or other statistical analyses, contact Medyan Statistics Consulting.

item.title: Wine Quality Prediction with R fastml Package: Automated ML Pipeline
item.dc:creator: Medyan Statistics
item.description:
Introduction

One of the most time-consuming steps in machine learning projects is training and comparing multiple models. The fastml package in R automates this process, allowing you to train, compare, and select the best model with a single function call.

In this post, we build a complete ML pipeline on the UCI Wine Quality (Red Wine) dataset.

Source code: github.com/bartuyurdacan/fastml_wine

Dataset

The dataset contains physicochemical properties and quality ratings of red wines:

  • Features: Fixed acidity, volatile acidity, citric acid, residual sugar, chlorides, free SO₂, total SO₂, density, pH, sulphates, alcohol
  • Target variable: Quality score (0-10)
Kodu Göster 
url_red <- "https://archive.ics.uci.edu/ml/machine-learning-databases/wine-quality/winequality-red.csv"
wine_red <- read.csv(url_red, sep = ";")

Model Training

With fastml, we train 5 different regression models in a single line:

Kodu Göster 
library(fastml)

model_fastml <- fastml(
  data = wine_red,
  label = "quality",
  algorithms = c("rand_forest", "xgboost", "elastic_net", "linear_reg", "lasso_reg")
)

This single call automatically:

  1. Splits data into train/test sets
  2. Preprocesses and scales features
  3. Trains each algorithm with hyperparameter tuning
  4. Calculates performance metrics
  5. Selects the best model

Model Comparison

Kodu Göster 
model_fastml$performance
Model RMSE R² MAE
Random Forest Lowest Highest Lowest
XGBoost Low High Low
Elastic Net Medium Medium Medium
Linear Reg High Low High
Lasso Reg High Low High

Residual Diagnostics

Visualizing the residual analysis of models:

Kodu Göster 
plot(model_fastml, type = "all")

Feature Importance and SHAP Explanations

Variable importance ranking and SHAP values with DALEX integration:

Kodu Göster 
vi <- explain_dalex(
  model_fastml,
  features = NULL,
  vi_iterations = 20
)

Results

  • Alcohol is the most important variable affecting wine quality
  • Higher volatile acidity decreases quality
  • Sulphates and citric acid contribute positively
  • Random Forest and XGBoost significantly outperform linear models

Reproducibility

The project uses renv for a fully reproducible environment:

Kodu Göster 
# Clone the project
# git clone https://github.com/bartuyurdacan/fastml_wine.git
# In R console:
renv::restore()
source("fastml.R")

For machine learning and statistical modeling projects, contact Medyan Statistics Consulting.

item.title: Publication-Ready Tables with gtsummary: Creating Table 1 in R
item.dc:creator: Medyan Statistics
item.description:
Introduction

Table 1 (baseline demographics table) is present in virtually every clinical and academic paper. Creating this table manually is time-consuming and error-prone.

The gtsummary package in R lets you create publication-ready tables with just a few lines of code.

Required Packages

Kodu Göster 
library(gtsummary)
library(dplyr)

Sample Data

Let’s create a simulated clinical trial dataset:

Kodu Göster 
set.seed(42)
n <- 200

df <- data.frame(
  age = round(c(rnorm(100, 55, 12), rnorm(100, 58, 10))),
  gender = sample(c("Male", "Female"), n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.55, 0.45)),
  bmi = round(c(rnorm(100, 26, 4), rnorm(100, 28, 5)), 1),
  smoking = sample(c("Yes", "No"), n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.3, 0.7)),
  diabetes = sample(c("Yes", "No"), n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.25, 0.75)),
  systolic_bp = round(c(rnorm(100, 128, 15), rnorm(100, 135, 18))),
  treatment = rep(c("Placebo", "Treatment"), each = 100)
)

Table 1: Demographics

Kodu Göster 
df |>
  select(age, gender, bmi, smoking, diabetes, systolic_bp, treatment) |>
  tbl_summary(
    by = treatment,
    label = list(
      age ~ "Age (years)",
      gender ~ "Gender",
      bmi ~ "BMI (kg/m²)",
      smoking ~ "Smoking",
      diabetes ~ "Diabetes",
      systolic_bp ~ "Systolic BP (mmHg)"
    ),
    statistic = list(
      all_continuous() ~ "{mean} ({sd})",
      all_categorical() ~ "{n} ({p}%)"
    ),
    digits = list(
      all_continuous() ~ 1,
      all_categorical() ~ c(0, 1)
    )
  ) |>
  add_p() |>
  add_overall() |>
  modify_header(label = "**Variable**") |>
  modify_spanning_header(c("stat_1", "stat_2") ~ "**Treatment Group**") |>
  bold_labels()
Variable Overall
N = 2001
Treatment Group
p-value2
Placebo
N = 1001		 Treatment
N = 1001
Age (years) 56.3 (10.9) 55.4 (12.5) 57.1 (9.0) 0.6
Gender


0.3
    Female 77 (38.5%) 35 (35.0%) 42 (42.0%)
    Male 123 (61.5%) 65 (65.0%) 58 (58.0%)
BMI (kg/m²) 26.8 (4.4) 26.1 (3.5) 27.4 (5.1) 0.036
Smoking 59 (29.5%) 28 (28.0%) 31 (31.0%) 0.6
Diabetes 47 (23.5%) 25 (25.0%) 22 (22.0%) 0.6
Systolic BP (mmHg) 130.8 (17.0) 125.5 (13.2) 136.1 (18.7) <0.001
1 Mean (SD); n (%)
2 Wilcoxon rank sum test; Pearson’s Chi-squared test

Regression Table

Presenting logistic regression results in a formatted table:

Kodu Göster 
df$outcome <- rbinom(n, 1, prob = 0.3 + 0.01 * (df$age - 50) + 0.05 * (df$treatment == "Treatment"))
Warning in rbinom(n, 1, prob = 0.3 + 0.01 * (df$age - 50) + 0.05 *
(df$treatment == : NAs produced
Kodu Göster 
model <- glm(outcome ~ age + gender + bmi + smoking + treatment,
             data = df, family = binomial)

model |>
  tbl_regression(
    exponentiate = TRUE,
    label = list(
      age ~ "Age",
      gender ~ "Gender",
      bmi ~ "BMI",
      smoking ~ "Smoking",
      treatment ~ "Treatment Group"
    )
  ) |>
  add_global_p() |>
  bold_p() |>
  bold_labels()
Characteristic OR 95% CI p-value
Age 1.02 0.99, 1.05 0.2
Gender

0.4
    Female — —
    Male 0.77 0.42, 1.43
BMI 0.97 0.91, 1.04 0.4
Smoking

0.9
    No — —
    Yes 0.96 0.49, 1.84
Treatment Group

0.3
    Placebo — —
    Treatment 1.41 0.77, 2.58
Abbreviations: CI = Confidence Interval, OR = Odds Ratio

Survival Table

Kodu Göster 
library(survival)

df$time <- rweibull(n, shape = 1.2, scale = 24)
df$status <- sample(0:1, n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.3, 0.7))

survfit(Surv(time, status) ~ treatment, data = df) |>
  tbl_survfit(
    times = c(6, 12, 18, 24),
    label_header = "**{time} Months**"
  )
Characteristic 6 Months 12 Months 18 Months 24 Months
treatment



    Placebo 88% (81%, 94%) 78% (70%, 87%) 61% (51%, 72%) 48% (39%, 60%)
    Treatment 84% (77%, 92%) 68% (60%, 78%) 65% (56%, 75%) 50% (41%, 62%)

Exporting Tables

Kodu Göster 
# Save in Word format
tbl <- df |>
  tbl_summary(by = treatment) |>
  add_p()

# Word
tbl |> as_flex_table() |> flextable::save_as_docx(path = "table1.docx")

# HTML
tbl |> as_gt() |> gt::gtsave("table1.html")

# LaTeX
tbl |> as_kable_extra(format = "latex")

Tips

  1. add_p(): Automatically selects the appropriate statistical test (t-test, Wilcoxon, chi-square, Fisher)
  2. add_overall(): Adds a total column
  3. bold_p(): Bolds significant p-values
  4. modify_footnote(): Add custom footnotes
  5. Journal format: Most journals accept Word tables - use as_flex_table()

Conclusion

gtsummary dramatically simplifies academic table creation. Instead of manual calculations and formatting, a few lines of R code produce publication-ready tables.

For academic table creation and statistical analysis support, contact Medyan Statistics Consulting.

item.title: Academic Charts in APA Format with ggplot2
item.dc:creator: Medyan Statistics
item.description:
Introduction

In academic papers, charts are critical for effective presentation of findings. The APA (American Psychological Association) format is the standard in many social science and health science journals.

In this post, we will create APA-compliant charts using ggplot2.

APA Chart Guidelines

  • Background: White (minimal grid lines)
  • Font: Sans-serif (Arial, Helvetica)
  • Color: Must be readable in black-and-white print
  • Axis labels: Clear and descriptive
  • Title: With figure number (Figure 1, Figure 2…)

Base APA Theme

Kodu Göster 
library(ggplot2)
Warning: package 'ggplot2' was built under R version 4.5.2
Kodu Göster 
library(dplyr)

# APA theme
theme_apa <- function(base_size = 12) {
  theme_classic(base_size = base_size) %+replace%
    theme(
      text = element_text(family = "sans"),
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = base_size + 2,
                                hjust = 0, margin = margin(b = 10)),
      axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
      axis.text = element_text(color = "black"),
      legend.position = "bottom",
      legend.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
      panel.grid = element_blank(),
      strip.background = element_blank(),
      strip.text = element_text(face = "bold")
    )
}

Example 1: Group Comparison (Bar Chart)

Kodu Göster 
set.seed(42)
df_bar <- data.frame(
  group = rep(c("Control", "Treatment A", "Treatment B"), each = 30),
  score = c(rnorm(30, 50, 10), rnorm(30, 62, 12), rnorm(30, 58, 11))
)

summary_df <- df_bar |>
  group_by(group) |>
  summarise(
    mean_score = mean(score),
    se = sd(score) / sqrt(n()),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(summary_df, aes(x = group, y = mean_score, fill = group)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6, color = "black", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean_score - se, ymax = mean_score + se),
                width = 0.15, linewidth = 0.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("gray80", "gray50", "gray30")) +
  labs(x = "Treatment Group", y = "Mean Score (±SE)") +
  theme_apa() +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 80))

Example 2: Box Plot

Kodu Göster 
ggplot(df_bar, aes(x = group, y = score, fill = group)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = 21, width = 0.5, color = "black") +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.1, alpha = 0.3, size = 1.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("gray90", "gray65", "gray40")) +
  labs(x = "Treatment Group", y = "Score") +
  theme_apa() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

Example 3: Scatter Plot + Regression

Kodu Göster 
set.seed(42)
df_scatter <- data.frame(
  study_hours = runif(80, 1, 10),
  exam_score = NA
)
df_scatter$exam_score <- 35 + 5.5 * df_scatter$study_hours + rnorm(80, 0, 8)

model <- lm(exam_score ~ study_hours, data = df_scatter)
r2 <- summary(model)$r.squared

ggplot(df_scatter, aes(x = study_hours, y = exam_score)) +
  geom_point(shape = 21, fill = "gray60", size = 2.5, stroke = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "black", fill = "gray85", linewidth = 0.8) +
  annotate("text", x = 2, y = 90,
           label = paste0("R² = ", round(r2, 3)),
           fontface = "italic", size = 4.5) +
  labs(x = "Daily Study Hours", y = "Exam Score") +
  theme_apa()
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Saving Charts

Kodu Göster 
# High-resolution saving (for journals)
ggsave("figure1.png", width = 8, height = 5, dpi = 300, bg = "white")
ggsave("figure1.tiff", width = 8, height = 5, dpi = 600, bg = "white")  # TIFF format
ggsave("figure1.pdf", width = 8, height = 5)  # Vector format

Conclusion

Creating APA-formatted charts with ggplot2 is possible with just a few lines of theme configuration. By defining your own theme_apa() function, you can achieve a consistent style across all your figures.

For academic charts and data visualization support, contact us.
